title: Why Do People Believe What They Believe?

required.title: [Read Aloud] In the Beginning: Creation Stories from Around the World
required.author: Virginia Hamilton

required.title: [Read Aloud] One World, Many Religions: The Ways We Worship
required.author: Mary Pope Osborne

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Myths and legends from specific traditions

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science
supplementary.author: Richard DeWitt

competence_markers.description: The student can explain what a worldview is in their own words
The student can retell at least three creation stories from different cultures, noting both differences and similarities
The student can name the big questions that worldviews answer (even if not all six, at least three or four)
The student can articulate the Golden Rule and explain that many cultures share some version of it
The student demonstrates the habit of asking "what did these people believe?" when hearing stories from different cultures
The student can narrate back someone else's belief accurately and fairly before offering their own reaction
The student has made a first attempt at identifying their own beliefs about at least one of the big questions
The student shows comfort hearing different answers to the same question without anxiety or dismissiveness

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about two different creation stories from two different cultures.
narration_prompts.detail: How were they different? Did they have anything in common?
Explain it as if you were telling a friend who has never heard the word before.

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick one of the big questions and tell me how two different cultures answered it.
narration_prompts.detail: Which answer do you find more interesting? Why?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the Golden Rule.
narration_prompts.detail: Why do so many different cultures have some version of it? What do you think that means?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does a sacred space look like?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick one you studied and describe it. What does it tell you about what those people believed?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me something you believe about the world.
narration_prompts.detail: Where did that belief come from? Did you choose it, or did you absorb it from somewhere?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do different cultures have different rules about right and wrong?
narration_prompts.detail: Does that mean there is no real right and wrong, or does it mean something else?

cross_connections.description: The creation stories and religious beliefs of Mesopotamia, Egypt, and other early civilizations are introduced in 1.1.1. This packet provides the framework for understanding what those beliefs reveal about the worldviews of the people who held them.
The Abrahamic stories encountered here — Genesis, the Ten Commandments — are developed in full in 1.3.2. This packet introduces the pattern; 1.3.2 fills in the traditions.
Hindu, Buddhist, and Confucian ideas touched on here are developed in 1.3.3. The student who has the worldview framework from this packet is prepared to engage Eastern traditions on their own terms.
The question of "who decides?" connects directly to political philosophy. Rules and authority are worldview questions that become governance questions.
The practice of examining one's own assumptions requires the self-reflective capacity built in IC1.1. The habits of honest self-assessment transfer directly.

content.why_this_matters:
Every person you have ever met — every person who has ever lived — carries around a set of invisible beliefs about the world. These beliefs answer the biggest questions there are: Where did everything come from? What are human beings? What is right and wrong? What happens when you die? What is life for?

Most people have never thought carefully about these beliefs. They absorbed them from their family, their community, their culture — the way you absorb language. You did not choose your first language. You grew up hearing it, and it became part of you. Beliefs about the world work the same way. Most people do not choose their beliefs about right and wrong, about God, about what matters most. They inherit them.

This packet is about learning to see those invisible beliefs — in the stories people tell, in the rules communities make, and eventually in yourself. You are going to hear stories from many different cultures. Every one of those stories was told by people who were trying to answer the same deep questions. Their answers are different. Some of their answers will surprise you. Some will sound familiar. The point is not to decide which one is right — not yet. The point is to notice that the questions are the same everywhere, and the answers are different everywhere, and that this is one of the most interesting things about being human.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The concept of a worldview** — the idea that everyone has invisible beliefs that shape how they see the world
2. **Recognition that every culture has creation stories** — and that these stories reveal what the culture values and fears
3. **The habit of asking "why?"** — not as defiance but as genuine curiosity about the reasons behind rules and stories
4. **The concept of right and wrong as something every culture cares about** — though the specific content differs
5. **Comfort with hearing different answers to the same question** — the beginning of intellectual tolerance without intellectual collapse
6. **The practice of narrating back what someone else believes** — accurately and fairly, before responding
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Invisible Glasses

Imagine you are wearing glasses that change the color of everything you see. If the lenses are blue, the whole world looks blue. You would not know the world was not blue unless you took the glasses off — or unless someone wearing red glasses told you the world looked different to them.

Everyone wears invisible glasses. These glasses are made of beliefs — beliefs about what is real, what is important, what is right and wrong, where everything came from, and what life is for. Your family has a set of these beliefs. Your community has a set. Every civilization in history has had a set.

We call this set of beliefs a **worldview.** A worldview is like a map of reality that tells you what to pay attention to, what to care about, and how to behave. Most people never look at their own map. They just follow it.

In this packet, you are going to start looking at the maps — other people's maps, and eventually your own.

### Part 2: The Big Questions That Everyone Asks

No matter where you go in the world — no matter how far back in time you travel — people have always asked the same big questions:

**Where did everything come from?** Every culture has a story about how the world began. The Sumerians said the god Marduk killed a chaos monster and made the world from her body. The Hebrews said God spoke the world into existence. The Norse said the world formed from the body of a frost giant. The Aboriginal Australians say the Ancestral Beings sang the world into being and are still singing it. These are very different answers — but the question is the same.

**What are human beings?** Are we special? Are we good or bad? Are we more than our bodies? Every culture has an answer. Some say we were made in the image of God. Some say we are part of nature, no more special than a tree or a river. Some say we have souls that live forever. Some say there is no permanent self at all. These answers shape everything about how a culture treats its people.

**What is right and wrong?** Every culture has rules. Every culture has ideas about what is good behavior and what is bad behavior. But the rules are different. Some cultures say it is wrong to eat certain foods. Some say it is wrong to charge interest on a loan. Some say it is wrong to own other human beings — but for most of human history, most cultures did not agree about that. The question of where right and wrong come from — God? Nature? The community? Reason? — is one of the deepest questions in the world.

**What happens when you die?** Some cultures say you go to a place of reward or punishment. Some say you are reborn as another living thing. Some say you join the ancestors and continue to influence the living. Some say nothing happens — you simply stop. The answer shapes how people live. A person who believes this life is all there is lives differently from a person who believes in a judgment after death.

**What is life for?** Is there a purpose? Is life about serving God? About being happy? About helping others? About achieving enlightenment? About building something that lasts? About survival? Every culture has an answer, and the answer shapes everything that culture builds.

### Part 3: Creation Stories — The Same Question, Many Answers

Let us look at how different cultures answered just one of these questions: *Where did everything come from?*

**The Hebrew creation story (Genesis):** God creates the world in six days by speaking it into existence. "Let there be light," and there is light. He creates the sky, the land, the plants, the animals, and finally human beings — made in God's own image. Then God rests on the seventh day. This story says that the world was made on purpose, by a God who is good, and that human beings are the most important part of creation.

**The Mesopotamian creation story (Enuma Elish):** The god Marduk battles the chaos goddess Tiamat and kills her. He splits her body in two — one half becomes the sky, the other the earth. Humans are created from the blood of a defeated god to serve the other gods, doing the work the gods no longer want to do. This story says humans exist to serve powers greater than themselves.

**The Hindu creation story:** In one version, the god Brahma creates the universe from himself — it emanates from him, exists within him, and will eventually return to him. The universe has been created and destroyed countless times. This story says the universe is not a one-time event but an eternal cycle.

**The Norse creation story:** The world forms in the gap between fire and ice. A frost giant, Ymir, emerges from the meeting of heat and cold. The gods kill Ymir and make the world from his body — his skull becomes the sky, his blood the oceans, his bones the mountains. This story says the world was made from violence and sacrifice, and it will end the same way.

**An Aboriginal Australian Dreaming story:** The Ancestral Beings moved across the land, and as they traveled, they created the features of the world — rivers, mountains, animals, plants. They are still present in the land. The world is not something that was made once in the past. It is being sustained right now by the sacred stories and ceremonies of the people who live on it.

Notice: Every one of these stories answers the same question. Every one gives a different answer. And every answer tells you something about what that culture valued, what it feared, and how it thought about the relationship between human beings and the world.

### Part 4: Rules and Where They Come From

Every family has rules. Do not hit. Share with your sibling. Say please and thank you. Go to bed on time. Clean up after yourself.

Have you ever asked *why* a rule exists? Not just "because I said so" — but the real reason behind it?

Cultures have rules too, and they disagree about where the rules come from:

**Some say the rules come from God.** The Jewish Torah contains 613 commandments that Jewish tradition says God gave to Moses on Mount Sinai. The Ten Commandments — do not murder, do not steal, do not lie — are the most famous. For a person who believes God gave these rules, breaking them is not just bad behavior. It is disobedience to the creator of the universe.

**Some say the rules come from nature.** Many traditions — including some Hindu, Confucian, and Stoic traditions — say that there is a natural order to the universe, and right behavior means living in harmony with that order. You can discover the rules by paying careful attention to how the world works.

**Some say the rules come from the community.** If the community agrees that something is right or wrong, then it is right or wrong for that community. Different communities can have different rules, and neither is wrong — they are just different. This is a common view today, and it raises a hard question: if one community says slavery is acceptable and another says it is monstrous, is there any way to say one is actually right?

**Some say the rules come from reason.** Human beings can figure out what is right and wrong by thinking carefully. You do not need God or tradition — you need logic and compassion. This view has produced some of the most important moral philosophy in history, and it has also produced some sharp disagreements about what reason actually requires.

The point here is not to decide which answer is correct. The point is to notice that the question exists — and that your own beliefs about where right and wrong come from are beliefs, not facts you discovered lying on the ground.

### Part 5: Good and Evil — A Universal Category

Here is something remarkable: every culture in the history of the world has a concept of good and evil. Every one. There are no exceptions.

The specific content differs. What counts as "good" in one culture may not count as "good" in another. But the structure — the idea that some things are right and some things are wrong, that some actions are noble and some are shameful, that human beings must choose between them — is universal.

The ancient Egyptians called the cosmic order Ma'at — truth, justice, balance. When you died, your heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at. If your heart was heavier than the feather — weighed down by wrongdoing — you were devoured by a monster.

The Zoroastrians said the world is a battlefield between Ahura Mazda (the god of truth and goodness) and Angra Mainyu (the spirit of lies and destruction). Every human must choose a side. Your thoughts, words, and deeds either strengthen the good or strengthen the evil. There is no neutral ground.

The Buddhists say the source of suffering is craving and ignorance. Right action — following the Eightfold Path — leads away from suffering. Wrong action deepens it.

The details are different. The structure is the same. Every culture says: some things are right, some things are wrong, and it matters which you choose.

### Part 6: The Golden Rule — Something Almost Everyone Agrees On

There is one moral idea that appears in almost every major tradition:

- **Judaism:** "What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbor." — Rabbi Hillel
- **Christianity:** "Do unto others as you would have them do unto you." — Jesus
- **Islam:** "No one of you is a believer until he desires for his brother that which he desires for himself." — Hadith
- **Hinduism:** "This is the sum of duty: do not do to others what would cause pain if done to you." — Mahabharata
- **Buddhism:** "Treat not others in ways that you yourself would find hurtful." — Udana-Varga
- **Confucianism:** "Do not do to others what you do not want done to yourself." — Confucius

This is called the Golden Rule, and some version of it appears in almost every culture and every era. This does not prove that morality comes from God, or from nature, or from reason. But it does suggest that human beings, everywhere, have arrived at similar moral intuitions about how people should treat each other. Why that is — whether it is evidence of a universal moral law, or simply the result of living in groups — is one of the great questions of philosophy.

### Part 7: What Is a Worldview? (Putting It Together)

A worldview is the set of answers a person or a culture gives to the big questions:

1. **What is real?** (Is there a God? Is the universe alive? Is there anything beyond what we can see and touch?)
2. **How do we know things?** (Do we trust sacred books? Reason? Experience? What the community teaches us?)
3. **What is a human being?** (Are we good? Bad? Both? Do we have souls? Are we special?)
4. **What is right and wrong?** (Where do moral rules come from? Are they the same for everyone?)
5. **What is life for?** (Is there a purpose? What are we supposed to do with our time on earth?)
6. **Who decides?** (God? The community? Each person for themselves? The government?)

Every person has answers to these questions — even if they have never thought about them. A person who says "I don't believe in anything" still has a worldview: they believe that the universe does not have a built-in purpose, that morality comes from somewhere other than God, and that human beings must figure things out on their own. Those are beliefs. They are a worldview.

The goal of this packet — and of everything you will study in 1.3 — is to learn to see worldviews. Your own. Other people's. The ones hidden inside the stories cultures tell and the rules they make. Once you can see them, you can think about them. And once you can think about them, you are free to choose your own deliberately, instead of just wearing whatever invisible glasses someone handed you.

### Part 8: Seeing the Story Behind the Story

When you hear a story — any story, from any culture — practice asking:

- What did the people who told this story believe about the world?
- What were they trying to explain?
- What did they think was important?
- What did they think was right and wrong?
- What does this story tell me about how they saw themselves?

This is not a trick. It is a skill. It is the skill of seeing the worldview behind the words. You will practice it with every story you hear in this curriculum — creation myths, hero tales, religious teachings, even the news. Every story has a worldview behind it. Learning to see it is one of the most powerful things a mind can do.

### Part 9: Your Own Invisible Glasses

Here is the hardest part: you have invisible glasses too.

You believe things about the world that you did not choose. You absorbed them from your family, your community, the books you read, the conversations you overheard. Some of those beliefs may be true. Some may not be. But right now, most of them are invisible to you — they feel like "just the way things are" rather than like beliefs you could examine.

Over the course of this curriculum, you are going to meet many different worldviews. Some will feel strange. Some will feel wrong. Some will feel obviously right. When something feels "obviously right," pay special attention — that is often the moment when your own invisible glasses are doing the most work. The things that feel most obviously true are often the things you have examined least.

This is not about doubting everything. It is about knowing what you believe and why — instead of just believing what you were handed.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Creation Stories Read-Through [Backbone of the Packet]
Read creation stories from *In the Beginning* by Virginia Hamilton aloud, one per session. After each story, the student narrates back: What happened in this story? Who made the world? How? Why? What does this story say about what human beings are? Keep a simple chart on the wall: the culture, how the world was made, what role humans have. By the end of the book, the chart shows the variety of human answers to the same question.

### Study 2: The Big Questions Wall [Early, Then Ongoing]
Write the six big questions (from Part 7) on a large poster. As you encounter stories and traditions throughout the packet, add how each tradition answers each question. This visual accumulation is the core learning tool — the student sees the pattern building over weeks.

### Study 3: The Golden Rule Collection [Mid-Packet]
After reading about the Golden Rule, the student collects as many versions as they can find. Write each one on a card with the tradition it comes from. Arrange them on a table. What do they have in common? Where do they differ? Why do so many different people in different places come up with the same idea?

### Study 4: Sacred Spaces [Mid-Packet]
Look at images of sacred spaces from at least five traditions — a Gothic cathedral, a mosque, a Hindu temple, a Shinto shrine, a synagogue, a stone circle, an Aboriginal sacred site. What do you notice? What does each space tell you about what the people who built it believed? Which one surprises you most? Why?

### Study 5: Rules and Reasons [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Pick three rules from three different traditions (e.g., the Sabbath in Judaism, the Five Precepts in Buddhism, the Confucian rules about respecting elders). For each, discuss: What is the rule? Why does this tradition have it? What does it tell you about what this tradition thinks is important? Could you imagine following this rule? Why or why not?

### Study 6: Tell Me What They Believe [Late Packet]
The mentor describes a worldview without naming it — just the beliefs. "These people believe the world was made by one God who is good. They believe human beings are the most important part of creation. They believe there are rules that God gave to people, and following them is the most important thing you can do." The student guesses which tradition it is. Then they try it: the student describes a worldview and the mentor guesses. This practices the skill of identifying worldviews by their structure.

### Study 7: My Own Glasses [End of Packet]
The student attempts to answer the six big questions for themselves. Not as a test — there are no wrong answers — but as an exercise in self-awareness. What do I believe about where the world came from? What do I think human beings are? What do I think is right and wrong? Where do my ideas come from? This is the beginning of conscious worldview examination, and it may be the most important conversation in the entire packet.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Worldview Concept
**Developing well:** The student can explain what a worldview is — that everyone has beliefs about the world, and those beliefs shape how they see everything. They may use the "invisible glasses" metaphor or create their own.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats the word "worldview" as vocabulary to memorize rather than a concept to use. They cannot apply it to a specific example.

**Diagnostic:** Have you connected the concept to specific stories? The concept becomes real only when applied: "What was the Egyptian worldview about what happens after death?"

### 2. Creation Story Engagement
**Developing well:** The student can retell multiple creation stories with detail and can identify what each story reveals about the culture that told it. They notice both similarities and differences across stories.

**Developing slowly:** The student remembers only one story, or remembers facts without understanding what the stories reveal about belief.

**Diagnostic:** Are you reading enough stories? The pattern only becomes visible through repetition. Five or six creation stories from different traditions is the minimum for the pattern to emerge.

### 3. The Big Questions
**Developing well:** The student can name at least three or four of the big questions and can give examples of different answers different cultures give.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot name any of the questions or treats them as a list to memorize rather than genuine questions people ask.

**Diagnostic:** Are you asking these questions in conversation, not just reading about them? "What do you think life is for?" asked at dinner is more powerful than reading about the question in a workbook.

### 4. Fair Representation
**Developing well:** The student narrates other people's beliefs accurately before offering their own opinion. When they disagree with a belief, they can still say what the believer actually believes.

**Developing slowly:** The student dismisses unfamiliar beliefs immediately: "That's weird" or "That's wrong." They cannot articulate what the other person actually believes.

**Diagnostic:** This skill requires modeling. When the student says "that creation story is dumb," respond with: "Tell me what the story says first — what did those people believe? Why did they believe it?" The habit of representing before reacting must be taught through practice, not instruction.

### 5. Self-Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can identify at least one belief they hold and can say where they think it came from. They show beginning awareness that their own beliefs are beliefs, not just facts.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats all of their own beliefs as obviously true and all unfamiliar beliefs as obviously strange.

**Diagnostic:** This is the most developmentally advanced skill in the packet and should not be forced. A six-year-old who says "I believe in God because my family does" has demonstrated genuine self-awareness. Do not push for more than the student can offer.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating This as Comparative Religion Already
This packet is not yet comparative religion — it is the prerequisite for comparative religion. The student is learning the framework (worldviews exist, they differ, they can be identified) that will be applied to specific traditions in 1.3.2 through 1.3.9. Do not rush into detailed theology. Stay with stories and questions.

### Signaling Which Worldview Is Correct
Even subtle signals — extra enthusiasm for your own tradition's creation story, a slight eye-roll when reading about karma — teach the student which worldview the mentor considers correct. Guard against this. The goal is not relativism but fairness. The student will form their own views; your job is to make sure they do so from a position of understanding, not inherited prejudice.

### Anxiety About Relativism
Some mentors worry that teaching children about multiple worldviews will make them relativists — unable to believe anything is true. The evidence suggests the opposite: students who understand multiple worldviews are better equipped to commit to their own, because their commitment is informed rather than inherited. A person who has never encountered a serious alternative to their own worldview holds it by default, not by choice. A person who has encountered many and chosen one holds it with genuine conviction.

### Skipping "My Own Glasses"
The final study — the student examining their own beliefs — is the most important and the easiest to skip. Do not skip it. It does not need to be long or deep. A five-minute conversation about "what do you think happens after you die, and where did that idea come from?" is sufficient. The point is to establish the practice, not to complete the analysis.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Two Creation Stories
**Strong:** Retells both stories with specific detail. Notes at least one difference and one similarity. May offer a personal reaction: "I liked the Norse one because it was dramatic."
**Weak:** "One was about God and one was about gods." No detail, no comparison.

### Prompt 2: What Is a Worldview?
**Strong:** Explains the concept clearly — beliefs about the world that everyone has, often without knowing it. May use the glasses metaphor or their own analogy.
**Weak:** "It's what you believe." True but insufficient.

### Prompt 3: A Big Question, Two Answers
**Strong:** Picks a specific question and gives two specific answers from two traditions. Shows understanding that the same question gets different answers.
**Weak:** Cannot pick a question or gives only one answer.

### Prompt 4: The Golden Rule
**Strong:** Explains the idea and notes that many cultures have it. May wonder why. May offer their own opinion about what it means.
**Weak:** "Be nice to people." No reference to the cross-cultural pattern.

### Prompt 5: A Sacred Space
**Strong:** Describes a specific space with detail — what it looks like, what it feels like, what it suggests about the beliefs of the people who built it.
**Weak:** "It was a church." No detail or interpretation.

### Prompt 6: A Personal Belief
**Strong:** Identifies a specific belief and can say something about where it came from — family, a book, a conversation, church, their own thinking.
**Weak:** Cannot identify any beliefs, or treats all beliefs as just obvious facts.
